content_en:
The personal status law section covers marriage, divorce, kinship, and related family matters — the domain of Islamic law most directly encountered by ordinary Muslim families. Dr. al-Zuhayli's treatment is particularly valuable here because personal status law is the area where the four schools differ most significantly and where contemporary Muslim communities most need reliable guidance.

On marriage, the author covers the conditions and pillars of a valid nikah contract across the four schools. One of the most practically significant differences is on the requirement of the wali (guardian): Hanafi law permits a legally competent adult woman to contract her own marriage without a wali, while the Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali schools require the wali as a condition of validity. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents the evidence — primarily the hadith 'there is no nikah without a wali' — and explains the Hanafi counter-argument based on the principle that a woman with full legal capacity may dispose of her own affairs. This difference has major implications for Muslim communities navigating family situations in non-Muslim countries.

The mahr (dower) section covers its types, the rules for its payment, the difference between prompt (mu'ajjal) and deferred (mu'ajjal) mahr, and what happens to the mahr in cases of divorce before and after consummation. The prohibition on the bride's family receiving or consuming the mahr is emphasized, as this practice — common in some cultures — contradicts the Quranic injunction to give women their mahr as a gift (4:4).

On divorce, the encyclopedia presents the schools' positions on the triple divorce pronounced in a single sitting — a topic of enormous practical consequence. The Hanafi, Maliki, and Shafi'i schools generally treat it as counting as three, while Ibn Taymiyyah and his school held it counts as one. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents both views carefully and objectively. The chapter also covers ila' (oath of abstinence), zihar (injurious comparison), li'an (mutual imprecation), and khul' — the complete landscape of Islamic marriage dissolution.
content_id:
Bagian hukum status pribadi mencakup pernikahan, perceraian, kekerabatan, dan hal-hal keluarga terkait — bidang hukum Islam yang paling langsung dihadapi keluarga Muslim biasa. Pembahasan Dr. al-Zuhayli sangat berharga di sini karena hukum status pribadi adalah bidang di mana keempat mazhab paling banyak berbeda dan di mana masyarakat Muslim kontemporer paling membutuhkan panduan yang dapat diandalkan.

Tentang pernikahan, pengarang membahas syarat-syarat dan rukun-rukun akad nikah yang sah di keempat mazhab. Salah satu perbedaan yang paling signifikan secara praktis adalah tentang persyaratan wali: hukum Hanafi mengizinkan perempuan dewasa yang cakap hukum untuk mengakadkan nikahnya sendiri tanpa wali, sementara mazhab Maliki, Syafi'i, dan Hanbali mensyaratkan wali sebagai syarat sahnya nikah. Dr. al-Zuhayli memaparkan dalil-dalilnya — terutama hadis "tidak ada nikah tanpa wali" — dan menjelaskan argumen tandingan Hanafi berdasarkan prinsip bahwa perempuan yang berkemampuan hukum penuh dapat mengurus urusannya sendiri. Perbedaan ini memiliki implikasi besar bagi masyarakat Muslim yang menavigasi situasi keluarga di negara-negara non-Muslim.

Bagian mahar mencakup jenis-jenisnya, aturan pembayarannya, perbedaan antara mahar mu'ajjal (segera) dan mu'ajjal (ditangguhkan), serta apa yang terjadi pada mahar dalam kasus cerai sebelum dan sesudah dukhul. Larangan keluarga mempelai perempuan menerima atau menggunakan mahar ditekankan, karena praktik ini — umum di beberapa budaya — bertentangan dengan perintah Al-Quran agar mahar diberikan kepada perempuan sebagai hadiah (4:4).

Tentang cerai, ensiklopedia ini memaparkan posisi mazhab-mazhab tentang talak tiga yang diucapkan sekaligus — topik yang sangat penting secara praktis. Mazhab Hanafi, Maliki, dan Syafi'i umumnya menganggapnya jatuh tiga, sementara Ibn Taimiyah dan mazhabnya berpendapat jatuh satu. Dr. al-Zuhayli memaparkan kedua pandangan ini secara cermat dan objektif. Bab ini juga mencakup ila', zihar, li'an, dan khul' — keseluruhan gambaran tentang pemutusan pernikahan dalam Islam.
content_ar: The personal status law الفصل covers marriage, divorce, kinship, and related الأسرة matters — the domain of Islamic law most directly encountered by ordinary مسلم families. Dr. al-Zuhayli's treatment is particularly valuable here لأن personal status law is the area where the four schools differ most significantly and where contemporary مسلم communities most need reliable الهداية. On marriage, the author covers the conditions and pillars of a valid nikah contract across the four schools. One of the most practically المهم differences is on the requirement of the wali (guardian): الحنفي law permits a legally competent adult woman to contract her own marriage without a wali, بينما the المالكي, الشافعي, and الحنبلي schools require the wali as a condition of validity. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents the evidence — primarily the الحديث 'there is no nikah without a wali' — and explains the الحنفي counter-argument based on the principle that a woman with full legal capacity may dispose of her own affairs. This difference has major implications for مسلم communities navigating الأسرة situations in non-مسلم countries. The mahr (dower) الفصل covers its types, the rules for its payment, the difference between prompt (mu'ajjal) and deferred (mu'ajjal) mahr, and what happens to the mahr in cases of divorce قبل and بعد consummation. The prohibition on the bride's الأسرة receiving or consuming the mahr is emphasized, as this practice — common in some cultures — contradicts the القرآني injunction to give women their mahr as a الهبة (4:4). On divorce, the encyclopedia presents the schools' positions on the triple divorce pronounced in a single sitting — a topic of enormous practical consequence. The الحنفي, المالكي, and الشافعي schools generally treat it as counting as three, بينما ابن تيمية and his school held it counts as one. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents both views carefully and objectively. The الباب أيضاً covers ila' (oath of abstinence), zihar (injurious comparison), li'an (mutual imprecation), and khul' — the complete landscape of Islamic marriage dissolution.
